news_summary: Today’s space news highlights groundbreaking discoveries and innovations, including fresh insights into the Big Bang, promising advancements with NASA’s Artemis Moon mission, and Webb telescope’s detection of an unprecedented cosmic explosion challenging current physics.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des découvertes et des innovations révolutionnaires, notamment de nouvelles perspectives sur le Big Bang, des avancées prometteuses concernant la mission lunaire Artemis de la NASA, ainsi que la détection par le télescope Webb d'une explosion cosmique sans précédent qui remet en question les théories physiques actuelles.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan descubrimientos e innovaciones revolucionarios, entre los que se incluyen nuevos datos sobre el Big Bang, avances prometedores en la misión lunar Artemis de la NASA y la detección por parte del telescopio Webb de una explosión cósmica sin precedentes que pone a prueba los principios de la física actual.

articles.title: A surprising new idea about how the Big Bang may have happened
articles.summary: Scientists at the University of Waterloo have uncovered a bold new way to explain how the universe began—one that could reshape our understanding of the Big Bang. Instead of relying on patched-together theories, their approach shows that the universe’s explosive early growth may arise naturally from a deeper framework called quantum gravity.
articles.content:
Scientists at the University of Waterloo have proposed a new way to explain how the universe began, offering a fresh perspective on the Big Bang and its earliest moments. Their findings suggest that the universe's rapid early expansion may have emerged naturally from a deeper and more complete theory known as quantum gravity.

The research was led by Dr. Niayesh Afshordi, a professor of physics and astronomy at the University of Waterloo and the Perimeter Institute (PI). His team explored a new way to combine gravity with quantum physics, which describes how the smallest particles behave. Although Einstein's theory of general relativity has worked extremely well for over a century, it fails under the extreme conditions present at the universe's birth. To overcome this, the researchers used Quadratic Quantum Gravity, a framework that remains mathematically stable even at the extremely high energies similar to those during the Big Bang.

A Simpler, More Unified Cosmic Model

Most current explanations of the Big Bang rely on general relativity along with additional elements introduced to make the models work. In contrast, this new approach provides a more unified picture, linking the universe's earliest moments directly to the well-tested models scientists use to study the cosmos today.

The team discovered that the universe's rapid early expansion can arise naturally from this consistent theory of quantum gravity, without the need for added assumptions. This expansion, known as inflation, is a key concept in cosmology because it helps explain the large-scale structure of the universe.

Testable Predictions and Gravitational Waves

The model also predicts a minimum level of primordial gravitational waves, which are tiny ripples in spacetime created shortly after the Big Bang. Future experiments may be able to detect these signals, giving scientists a rare opportunity to test ideas about the universe's quantum beginnings.

"This work shows that the universe's explosive early growth can come directly from a deeper theory of gravity itself," Afshordi said. "Instead of adding new pieces to Einstein's theory, we found that the rapid expansion emerges naturally once gravity is treated in a way that remains consistent at extremely high energies."

From Theory to Observable Evidence

The researchers were surprised by how testable their ideas turned out to be.

"Even though this model deals with incredibly high energies, it leads to clear predictions that today's experiments can actually look for," Afshordi said. "That direct link between quantum gravity and real data is rare and exciting."

A New Era of Precision Cosmology

This work arrives at a time when cosmology is becoming increasingly precise. New instruments are now capable of measuring the universe with unprecedented accuracy. Upcoming galaxy surveys, cosmic microwave background studies, and gravitational wave detectors are reaching the sensitivity needed to examine ideas that were once purely theoretical. At the same time, scientists are recognizing the limits of simpler models of early universe expansion, highlighting the need for approaches grounded in fundamental physics.

Looking Ahead

The study also involved Ruolin Liu, a PhD student at Waterloo and PI, and Dr. Jerome Quintin, a lecturer at l'École de technologie supérieure and a former postdoctoral researcher at Waterloo and PI. The team plans to refine its predictions for future experiments and investigate how this framework connects to particle physics and other unanswered questions about the early universe. Their long-term goal is to build a stronger link between quantum gravity and observable cosmology.

The paper, "Ultraviolet completion of the Big Bang in quadratic gravity," appears in Physical Review Letters.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding the Big Bang through a new quantum gravity framework, offering a more unified and testable model of the universe's origin. This has broad implications for cosmology and fundamental physics, potentially reshaping our understanding of the universe and enabling future experimental verification.
articles.personality_title: Scientists propose new quantum gravity model to explain the Big Bang
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied the Big Bang, the event that started our universe. Traditional explanations use Einstein’s theory of gravity, but this theory struggles to explain what happened at the very beginning.

**What happened** – A team at the University of Waterloo, led by Dr. Niayesh Afshordi, developed a new way to explain the Big Bang using a theory called Quadratic Quantum Gravity. This theory combines gravity with quantum physics, which studies tiny particles, in a way that works even at the extremely high energies present during the universe’s birth. Their model shows that the universe’s rapid early growth, called inflation, can happen naturally without adding extra assumptions.

**Impact** – This is important because it offers a simpler and more complete explanation of how the universe began. Unlike older models that add extra parts to Einstein’s theory, this approach uses one consistent framework. It also predicts the existence of primordial gravitational waves—tiny ripples in space and time—that future experiments might detect to test the theory.

**What's next step** – The researchers will refine their predictions and work on connecting their model to other areas like particle physics. Upcoming space and ground experiments, including gravitational wave detectors, will try to find the signs their theory predicts. This could help scientists confirm or improve our understanding of the universe’s earliest moments.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new quantum gravity model from the University of Waterloo offers a clear, testable explanation of the Big Bang’s rapid expansion, potentially changing how we understand the universe’s origin.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques proposent un nouveau modèle de gravité quantique pour expliquer le Big Bang
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient depuis longtemps le Big Bang, l’événement qui a donné naissance à notre univers. Les explications traditionnelles utilisent la théorie de la gravité d’Einstein, mais cette théorie a du mal à expliquer ce qui s’est passé au tout début.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de l’Université de Waterloo, dirigée par le Dr Niayesh Afshordi, a développé une nouvelle façon d’expliquer le Big Bang en utilisant une théorie appelée gravité quantique quadratique. Cette théorie combine la gravité avec la physique quantique, qui étudie les plus petites particules, d’une manière qui fonctionne même aux énergies très élevées présentes lors de la naissance de l’univers. Leur modèle montre que la croissance rapide de l’univers au début, appelée inflation, peut se produire naturellement sans ajouter d’hypothèses supplémentaires.

**Impact** – Cela est important car cela offre une explication plus simple et complète de la façon dont l’univers a commencé. Contrairement aux anciens modèles qui ajoutent des éléments à la théorie d’Einstein, cette approche utilise un cadre cohérent. Elle prédit aussi l’existence d’ondes gravitationnelles primordiales – de minuscules ondulations dans l’espace-temps – que les futures expériences pourraient détecter pour tester la théorie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont affiner leurs prédictions et travailler à relier leur modèle à d’autres domaines comme la physique des particules. Les expériences à venir, dans l’espace et au sol, y compris les détecteurs d’ondes gravitationnelles, tenteront de trouver les signes que leur théorie prédit. Cela pourrait aider les scientifiques à confirmer ou améliorer notre compréhension des tout premiers instants de l’univers.

**Phrase clé** – Un nouveau modèle de gravité quantique de l’Université de Waterloo offre une explication claire et testable de l’expansion rapide du Big Bang, pouvant changer notre compréhension de l’origine de l’univers.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos proponen nuevo modelo de gravedad cuántica para explicar el Big Bang
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado durante mucho tiempo el Big Bang, el evento que dio origen a nuestro universo. Las explicaciones tradicionales usan la teoría de la gravedad de Einstein, pero esta teoría no explica bien lo que pasó al principio.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de la Universidad de Waterloo, liderado por el Dr. Niayesh Afshordi, desarrolló una nueva forma de explicar el Big Bang usando una teoría llamada gravedad cuántica cuadrática. Esta teoría combina la gravedad con la física cuántica, que estudia las partículas más pequeñas, de una manera que funciona incluso con las energías muy altas que había cuando nació el universo. Su modelo muestra que el crecimiento rápido del universo al principio, llamado inflación, puede ocurrir de forma natural sin agregar suposiciones extras.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque ofrece una explicación más simple y completa de cómo comenzó el universo. A diferencia de modelos anteriores que añaden partes a la teoría de Einstein, este enfoque usa un marco consistente. También predice la existencia de ondas gravitacionales primordiales, pequeñas ondas en el espacio y tiempo, que futuros experimentos podrían detectar para probar la teoría.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores mejorarán sus predicciones y trabajarán para conectar su modelo con otras áreas como la física de partículas. Experimentos futuros, en el espacio y en tierra, incluyendo detectores de ondas gravitacionales, intentarán encontrar las señales que su teoría predice. Esto podría ayudar a los científicos a confirmar o mejorar nuestra comprensión de los primeros momentos del universo.

**Frase clave** – Un nuevo modelo de gravedad cuántica de la Universidad de Waterloo ofrece una explicación clara y comprobable de la rápida expansión del Big Bang, que podría cambiar cómo entendemos el origen del universo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an abstract cosmic scene where a radiant, expanding sphere made of intertwined glowing threads symbolizes quantum gravity, surrounded by delicate ripples representing primordial gravitational waves, set against a soft, dark backdrop evoking the early universe’s birth and rapid inflation.

articles.title: Countdown to Artemis: is NASA’s Moon mission the dawn of a new space age?
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 30 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00998-8As humans, we must be intentional about whether, and how, we explore space.
articles.content:
A NASA satellite’s camera captured the far side of the Moon as it passed in front of Earth.Credit: NASA/NOAA

Curiosity draws us to the cosmos, and as a planetary scientist, I am excited about the upcoming Artemis II mission. With its crewed trip around the Moon, humanity is poised to leave Earth orbit for the first time since 1972.

It might also be the point at which humanity decides to commit to becoming a space-faring civilization — and to the decades of hard work that this will require.

How to keep astronauts healthy in deep space

The Moon remains compelling for further exploration, even with the knowledge gained from the Apollo missions and more than 50 years of robotic mission data. Its surface offers the Solar System’s most pristine record of how a planetary crust forms and evolves. It provides evidence of asteroid and comet hits that are key to understanding the history of Earth and the inner Solar System. We now know that the Moon hosts water, although we don’t understand where it comes from, how much there is and whether, on an airless body, it cycles from solid to vapour like it does on Earth.

In the longer term, the Artemis programme aims to set up a permanent base on the Moon, which could become a step towards sending humans to Mars. Although the programme is driven now by technical goals, the scope for science is huge. Any researcher in the world could study the rock and ice samples that might be routinely brought back. A radio telescope built on the lunar far side, shielded from electronic signals from Earth, would enable astronomers to peer into the cosmic dark ages. Exciting as the Moon is, we won’t find life there. But Mars has the potential to harbour signs of life in its thick snowpacks, ancient rock strata and possible aquifers.

Yet even with this future in reach, it is not a given. Any human mission is risky. Artemis II will be a success only when the astronauts return to Earth. And then will come the most fragile parts of the US Moon–Mars plan — the technological, programmatic and societal aspects.

‘I’m touching space’: the fascinating insights meteorites can bring us

Technologically, landing on another planet is hard. Most first attempts fail. NASA recently pivoted its next mission, Artemis III, to in-space tests rather than a lunar landing. This adds a wise dose of realistic engineering milestones to the Artemis programme. It also gives NASA and industry more time to build up to the cadence of missions required to deliver crew and infrastructure to the Moon regularly.
articles.reasoning: The article discusses NASA's Artemis II mission, marking humanity's return to crewed lunar exploration for the first time since 1972, with broad implications for space science, technology, and future human space colonization. It highlights significant scientific opportunities, technological challenges, and the potential for long-term human presence on the Moon and eventual Mars exploration, which represents a meaningful and inspiring advancement for humanity.
articles.personality_title: NASA's Artemis II mission brings humans back to the Moon after 50 years
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Since 1972, humans have not traveled beyond Earth's orbit. NASA is preparing the Artemis II mission to send astronauts around the Moon, marking the first crewed lunar journey in more than 50 years. This mission is part of a larger plan to explore space and possibly live beyond Earth.

**What happened** – NASA will soon launch Artemis II, a spacecraft carrying astronauts on a trip around the Moon. This mission follows many years of robotic missions and scientific studies of the Moon. Artemis II itself will not land on the Moon but will test important space travel technologies needed for future missions.

**Impact** – Artemis II is important because it restarts human exploration beyond Earth’s orbit. The Moon has special scientific value: it holds clues about how planets form and has water in places we don’t fully understand yet. Learning more could help build a permanent base on the Moon. Such a base might be the first step toward sending people to Mars, where signs of past life could exist.

**What's next step** – After Artemis II, NASA plans other missions like Artemis III that aim to land astronauts on the Moon. These missions will test new technology and space travel methods. NASA wants to create regular trips to the Moon and build infrastructure for humans to live there longer.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Artemis II marks a new chapter in human space exploration, testing the path toward living on the Moon and eventually exploring Mars.
articles.personality_title_fr: La mission Artemis II de la NASA ramène les humains sur la Lune après 50 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis 1972, les humains n'ont pas voyagé au-delà de l'orbite terrestre. La NASA prépare la mission Artemis II pour envoyer des astronautes autour de la Lune, marquant le premier voyage habité lunaire depuis plus de 50 ans. Cette mission fait partie d'un plan plus large pour explorer l’espace et peut-être vivre au-delà de la Terre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La NASA va bientôt lancer Artemis II, un vaisseau spatial transportant des astronautes pour un voyage autour de la Lune. Cette mission suit de nombreuses années de missions robotiques et d’études scientifiques de la Lune. Artemis II ne se posera pas sur la Lune mais testera des technologies importantes pour les futures missions.

**Impact** – Artemis II est importante car elle relance l’exploration humaine au-delà de l’orbite terrestre. La Lune a une valeur scientifique spéciale : elle contient des indices sur la formation des planètes et de l’eau dans des endroits encore peu compris. En apprendre plus pourrait aider à construire une base permanente sur la Lune, première étape vers l’envoi d’humains sur Mars, où des signes de vie passée pourraient exister.

**Prochaine étape** – Après Artemis II, la NASA prévoit d’autres missions comme Artemis III visant à poser des astronautes sur la Lune. Ces missions testeront de nouvelles technologies et méthodes de voyage spatial. La NASA veut créer des voyages réguliers vers la Lune et construire une infrastructure pour que les humains y vivent plus longtemps.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Artemis II marque un nouveau chapitre dans l’exploration spatiale humaine, testant la voie vers la vie sur la Lune et l’exploration future de Mars.
articles.personality_title_es: La misión Artemis II de la NASA lleva a humanos de vuelta a la Luna tras 50 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Desde 1972, los humanos no han viajado más allá de la órbita terrestre. La NASA está preparando la misión Artemis II para enviar astronautas alrededor de la Luna, marcando el primer viaje tripulado lunar en más de 50 años. Esta misión es parte de un plan más grande para explorar el espacio y posiblemente vivir fuera de la Tierra.

**Qué pasó** – La NASA lanzará pronto Artemis II, una nave espacial con astronautas que harán un viaje alrededor de la Luna. Esta misión sigue muchos años de misiones robóticas y estudios científicos sobre la Luna. Artemis II no aterrizará en la Luna, pero probará tecnologías importantes para futuras misiones.

**Impacto** – Artemis II es importante porque reinicia la exploración humana más allá de la órbita terrestre. La Luna tiene un valor científico especial: guarda pistas sobre cómo se forman los planetas y tiene agua en lugares que aún no comprendemos bien. Aprender más podría ayudar a construir una base permanente en la Luna, un primer paso para enviar personas a Marte, donde podrían existir señales de vida pasada.

**Próximo paso** – Después de Artemis II, la NASA planea otras misiones como Artemis III que buscarán aterrizar astronautas en la Luna. Estas misiones probarán nuevas tecnologías y métodos de viaje espacial. La NASA quiere realizar viajes regulares a la Luna y construir infraestructura para que los humanos vivan allí más tiempo.

**Frase clave** – Artemis II marca un nuevo capítulo en la exploración espacial humana, probando el camino hacia vivir en la Luna y explorar Marte en el futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a luminous Moon with its far side softly illuminated, orbiting a vibrant Earth in the background, connected by a glowing, arcing path symbolizing the Artemis II crewed mission, set against a deep starry sky with subtle hints of radio waves and scientific instruments abstractly woven into the lunar surface.

articles.title: Webb telescope spots mysterious explosion that defies known physics
articles.summary: Astronomers have spotted a bizarre cosmic explosion that refuses to play by the rules—and it’s leaving scientists scrambling for answers. GRB 250702B, detected by NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope and a global network of observatories, lasted an astonishing seven hours—far longer than typical gamma-ray bursts, which usually fade in under a minute.
articles.content:
A Rutgers astrophysicist is helping investigate a strange cosmic event that has left astronomers searching for answers. At the center of the mystery is an unusually powerful explosion in space that lasted far longer than anything previously observed.

NASA announced that researchers using the James Webb Space Telescope examined GRB 250702B, a long gamma-ray burst and one of the most energetic types of events in the universe. These bursts typically occur when a massive star collapses into a black hole, producing a brief and intense flash of high-energy gamma rays. This event behaved very differently.

"This object shows extreme properties that are difficult to explain," said Huei Sears, a postdoctoral researcher in the Department of Physics and Astronomy at the Rutgers School of Arts and Sciences who is studying the explosion. "Usually, these bursts are over in less than a minute, but GRB 250702B lasted for hours and even showed signs of X-ray activity a day prior."

Global Observations Reveal Unusual Behavior

Sears explained that observatories around the world are analyzing data from the event. This includes teams working with China's Einstein Probe and the National Science Foundation's Very Large Array, which is widely recognized from its appearance in the science fiction film Contact.

The gamma-ray emission continued for at least seven hours, nearly doubling the duration of the previous record holder. NASA also released an animation showing one possible scenario for the event. In this model, a black hole about three times the mass of the Sun, with an event horizon just 11 miles (18 kilometers) wide, orbits and merges with a companion star.

"This is certainly an outburst unlike any other we have seen in the past 50 years," said Eliza Neights, an astronomer at NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center in Maryland.

Possible Explanations Involving Black Holes

Scientists are considering several explanations. One possibility is that this was an unusually extreme gamma-ray burst. Another is that it was a tidal disruption event, in which a black hole thousands of times more massive than the Sun tears apart a star that ventured too close. A more unusual idea suggests a smaller black hole merged with a stripped helium star and consumed it from within.

Regardless of the exact cause, the black hole did far more than take a small bite. It unleashed powerful jets of energy that shot across space.

Multi-Telescope Effort Captures the Event

NASA's Fermi Gamma-ray Space Telescope first detected the burst on July 2, prompting rapid follow-up observations from other instruments. The event was so intense that no single telescope could capture the full picture. Scientists combined data from space-based and ground-based observatories, collecting gamma rays, X-rays, infrared light, and radio signals. The explosion was not visible in ordinary light.

"Only through the combined power of instruments on multiple spacecraft could we understand this event," said Eric Burns, an astrophysicist at Louisiana State University.

Distant Galaxy Adds to the Mystery

Images from the Hubble Space Telescope revealed an unusual galaxy at the location of the burst. Initially, it appeared as though two galaxies might be merging, or that a single galaxy was split by a dark band of dust. Later, Webb observations showed the galaxy lies about 8 billion light-years away, meaning the explosion occurred long before Earth formed.

To better understand the host galaxy, Sears led follow-up observations using Webb's NIRCam, its main near-infrared imaging instrument, several months after the event.

"In such vibrant and unprecedented detail, we see just one very large galaxy with a dust lane," Sears said. "The galaxy has such complex structure that it's not 100% clear if there's anything left to see of the explosion, but if there is, it's really faint."

Mystery Remains Unsolved

This finding supports the idea that GRB 250702B was a gamma-ray burst rather than a tidal disruption event. Even so, researchers have not reached a definitive conclusion.

"We have only seen a few tidal disruption events of this type, so we don't know for sure how they're supposed to evolve," Sears said. "A lot of the studies on this explosion provide different, and sometimes contradictory, explanations. It's still early in our understanding of what really happened."

Whatever the final explanation, scientists agree the event is both rare and significant.

"This gives us a unique chance to study the extremes of how stars and black holes evolve," Sears said. "GRB 250702B could even be the discovery of something unexpected and new."

The Webb telescope also is supported by the European Space Agency (ESA), and the Canadian Space Agency (CSA).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of a mysterious cosmic explosion detected by the James Webb Space Telescope and other observatories, revealing new phenomena in astrophysics. This event has broad implications for understanding the universe, involves a global scientific effort, and offers potential breakthroughs in knowledge about black holes and gamma-ray bursts, inspiring curiosity and hope for future discoveries.
articles.personality_title: Webb telescope finds unusually long cosmic explosion challenging scientists
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study powerful cosmic explosions called gamma-ray bursts, which usually last less than a minute and happen when massive stars collapse into black holes.

**What happened** – On July 2, NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope and other observatories detected GRB 250702B, a gamma-ray burst that lasted an extraordinary seven hours. This event showed unusual energy patterns and started with X-ray activity a day before the main explosion.

**Impact** – This burst lasted much longer than any seen before, nearly doubling the previous record. It challenges what scientists know about how stars and black holes behave. The event took place in a galaxy 8 billion light-years away, long before Earth existed, and involved complex interactions possibly between black holes and stars.

**What's next step** – Researchers worldwide are analyzing data from many telescopes to better understand the cause. They hope to learn if this event is a rare type of gamma-ray burst, a black hole tearing apart a star, or a new kind of cosmic occurrence. Further observations with the Webb telescope and others will continue to explore these questions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The James Webb Space Telescope discovered a cosmic explosion much longer than normal, opening new paths to understand mysterious space events.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le télescope Webb découvre une explosion cosmique exceptionnellement longue qui intrigue les scientifiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient des explosions cosmiques puissantes appelées sursauts gamma, qui durent généralement moins d'une minute et se produisent lorsque des étoiles massives s'effondrent en trous noirs.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le 2 juillet, le télescope spatial James Webb de la NASA et d'autres observatoires ont détecté GRB 250702B, un sursaut gamma qui a duré sept heures, ce qui est exceptionnel. Cet événement a montré des signes d'énergie inhabituels et a commencé par une activité en rayons X un jour avant l'explosion principale.

**Impact** – Ce sursaut a duré beaucoup plus longtemps que tous ceux observés auparavant, doublant presque le précédent record. Il remet en question les connaissances des scientifiques sur le comportement des étoiles et des trous noirs. L'explosion a eu lieu dans une galaxie à 8 milliards d'années-lumière, bien avant la formation de la Terre, et implique des interactions complexes entre trous noirs et étoiles.

**Prochaine étape** – Des chercheurs du monde entier analysent les données des nombreux télescopes pour mieux comprendre la cause. Ils espèrent déterminer si cet événement est un type rare de sursaut gamma, un trou noir déchirant une étoile, ou un phénomène cosmique nouveau. D'autres observations avec Webb et d'autres instruments continueront à explorer ces questions.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le télescope spatial James Webb a découvert une explosion cosmique beaucoup plus longue que la normale, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour comprendre des phénomènes spatiaux mystérieux.
articles.personality_title_es: El telescopio Webb detecta una explosión cósmica inusualmente larga que desconcierta a los científicos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian explosiones cósmicas poderosas llamadas estallidos de rayos gamma, que normalmente duran menos de un minuto y ocurren cuando estrellas masivas colapsan en agujeros negros.

**Qué pasó** – El 2 de julio, el telescopio espacial James Webb de la NASA y otros observatorios detectaron GRB 250702B, un estallido de rayos gamma que duró siete horas, algo extraordinario. Este evento mostró patrones de energía inusuales y comenzó con actividad de rayos X un día antes de la explosión principal.

**Impacto** – Este estallido duró mucho más que cualquier otro registrado, casi el doble del récord anterior. Esto desafía lo que los científicos saben sobre cómo se comportan las estrellas y los agujeros negros. El evento ocurrió en una galaxia a 8 mil millones de años luz, mucho antes de que existiera la Tierra, e involucró interacciones complejas posiblemente entre agujeros negros y estrellas.

**Próximo paso** – Investigadores de todo el mundo están analizando datos de varios telescopios para entender mejor la causa. Esperan saber si este evento es un tipo raro de estallido de rayos gamma, un agujero negro desmembrando una estrella, o un fenómeno cósmico nuevo. Continuarán las observaciones con el telescopio Webb y otros para explorar estas preguntas.

**Frase clave** – El telescopio espacial James Webb descubrió una explosión cósmica mucho más larga de lo normal, abriendo nuevas formas de entender eventos misteriosos en el espacio.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a glowing, swirling cosmic explosion with radiant jets of energy bursting outward from a small, dark silhouette of a black hole orbiting a luminous, partially consumed star, set against the backdrop of a vast, intricately textured galaxy with a distinct dark dust lane, all rendered in warm, natural hues of deep blues, soft oranges, and gentle purples.
